introduction: Clock problems test your ability to relate the motion of the hour and minute hands on a 12-hour dial. The minute hand completes 360° in 60 minutes while the hour hand moves only 30° in that time, so their relative speed is 5.5° per minute.
explanation:
CONCEPT EXPLANATION

Minute hand speed = 6°/min. Hour hand speed = 0.5°/min. Relative speed = 5.5°/min.

Angle at H:M → θ = |30H − 5.5M|°. Use 360° − θ if θ > 180° for the smaller angle.

Hands coincide after H o'clock at 60H/11 minutes past H.

Hands opposite: solve |30H − 5.5M| = 180.

Mirror time: actual = 11:60 − mirror reading (in minutes on 12-hour dial).

Faulty clock: ratio of faulty time to true time stays constant.

REAL-LIFE APPLICATIONS

Scheduling, analog display design, gear-ratio analysis, and mirror/reflection puzzles.

COMMON MISTAKES

1) Using 30M instead of 5.5M.
2) Ignoring hour-hand movement with minutes.
3) Not reducing reflex angles.
4) Assuming coincidence every 60 minutes.
5) Wrong mirror subtraction base.

IMPORTANT NOTES

• 11 coincidences and 11 opposites in 12 hours.
• 22 right angles in 12 hours.
• Coincidence interval = 720/11 ≈ 65.45 min.
• Minute hand is 12× faster than hour hand.

shortcutTips.title: 5.5° rule
shortcutTips.tip: Each minute the minute hand gains 5.5° on the hour hand.

shortcutTips.title: At H:00
shortcutTips.tip: Angle at H:00 is simply 30H degrees.

shortcutTips.title: 12× speed
shortcutTips.tip: Minute hand moves 12 times as fast as the hour hand.
